elements.sent-2411-065: Here we outline conventions and notations for heaps and heap predicates, as well as our choice of concrete heap semantics.
elements.sent-2411-066: A heap is a finite partial map from addresses (\L) to concrete values (\V).
elements.sent-2411-067: Denote the set of all heaps with \H, the set of non-null values as $\V^* = \V \setminus \set{\vnull}$, and the finite address domain of heap $h$ with $\domain{h}$.
elements.sent-2411-068: Concrete values may be any typical datatypes (integers, booleans, pairs, etc.) which are generally fixed in size (e.g.\ an unbounded list would not typically be stored as one concrete value).
elements.sent-2411-069: Addresses themselves can be treated as concrete values.
elements.sent-2411-070: Assume unlimited addresses are available, namely $\forall h.\, \domain{h \subsetneq \L$.}
elements.sent-2411-071: Heaps $h_1$ and $h_2$ are disjoint ($h_1 \disjoint h_2$) iff $\domain{h_1 \disjoint \domain{h_2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-072: The union of disjoint heaps ($h_1 \union h_2$) yields the combined partial map of $h_1$ and $h_2$.
elements.sent-2411-073: Dually, a heap may be partitioned into smaller disjoint heaps.
elements.sent-2411-075: A heap predicate characterizes the addresses and values of heaps.
elements.sent-2411-076: Given heap predicate $\Psi$ on \H, $\Psi(h)$ denotes that $h$ satisfies $\Psi$, and $\H(\Psi)$ denotes the set of all $\Psi$-satisfying heaps.
elements.sent-2411-077: Sometimes we parameterize a heap predicate on some domain $V$.
elements.sent-2411-078: In this case, $\Psi(v,h)$ denotes that $h$ satisfies $\Psi$ with $v \in V$.
elements.sent-2411-079: Aside from heap predicates containing pure logical expressions and quantifiers, we will describe heap contents directly with two standard predicates from separation logic \cite{reynolds_sep_logic,Parkinson .}
elements.sent-2411-080: The points-to predicate $p \mapsto v$, for $p \in \L$ and $v \in \V$, holds for $h$ iff $\domain{h = \set{p}$ and $h(p) = v$.}
elements.sent-2411-081: The separating conjunction operator $\Psi \ast \Phi$, for predicates $\Psi$ and $\Phi$, holds for $h$ iff $h = h_1 + h_2$ for some $h_1$ and $h_2$ s.t.\ $h_1 \disjoint h_2$ and $\Psi(h_1)$ and $\Phi(h_2)$.
elements.sent-2411-082: We will also want to characterize when a portion of a heap satisfies a particular predicate.
elements.sent-2411-083: For this, we define heap predicate extension:.
elements.sent-2411-084: For predicate $\Psi$, $h$ satisfies extension $\Psi^+$ iff a subheap of $h$ satisfies $\Psi$.
elements.sent-2411-086: We choose to represent concrete programs as total functions over heaps, so that our semantics are highly general, and thus broadly applicable; we admit semantics which are nondeterministic, but must be constructive and must terminate.
elements.sent-2411-087: Nondeterminism mainly arises during address allocation, though this work is agnostic to the source of nondeterminism.
elements.sent-2411-088: A (concrete) program is a total function $f : \H \ra \pset\H$, subject to the following properties:.
elements.sent-2411-089: On any initial heap $h$, $f$ terminates or fails in finite time.
elements.sent-2411-090: If $f$ successfully terminates on $h$, then $f(h)$ yields the set of all possible final heaps in which $f$ may terminate.
elements.sent-2411-093: Namely, if $f(h) \neq \emptyset$, then for any $h' \disjoint h$: $f(h \union h') \neq \emptyset$ and $\forall k \in f(h \union h').\ h' \subseteq k$.
elements.sent-2411-094: Local action enforces that $f$ does not modify data outside its footprint, as a weak form of the typical frame rule of separation logic~\cite{reynolds_sep_logic .}
elements.sent-2411-095: For any particular semantics that satisfies framing by construction, the local action constraint should immediately hold for all concrete programs.
elements.sent-2411-096: When working with compound abstractions, we will define a stronger framing property (Def.~\ref{def:capture ).}
elements.sent-2411-097: For two programs to commute, they must terminate without failure in the first place.
elements.sent-2411-098: To that end, we classify the initial heaps upon which $f$ can execute.
elements.sent-2411-099: Sufficient heaps contain the necessary addresses, which map to appropriate values, for $f$ to run.
elements.sent-2411-100: In contrast, heaps in the footprint of $f$ contain appropriate addresses, but may not contain appropriate values; thus a heap in $f$'s footprint on which $f$ fails cannot be ``corrected'' with further allocations.
elements.sent-2411-101: Precisely: \begin{equation}\sufficient{f} \triangleq \setbuild{h}{f(h) \neq \emptyset} \footprint{f} \triangleq \sufficient{f} \cup \setbuild{h}{\forall h' \disjoint h.\, h \union h' \notin \sufficient{f}}\end{equation} Our concrete domain, as the counterpart to our eventual abstract domain, will be $\sC \triangleq \pset\H$.
elements.sent-2411-102: Concrete transformers over this domain map from sets of heaps to sets of heaps.
elements.sent-2411-103: We derive these transformers directly from programs:.
elements.sent-2411-104: The concrete transformer constructed from $f$, denoted $\bar f$, is defined as $\bar f(C) = \bigcup_{h\in C f(h)$ if $C \subseteq \sufficient{f}$, else $\emptyset$.}
elements.sent-2411-105: Relatedly, we define the composition/sequence of two programs so that the second program fails entirely if it fails on any one output of the first program; namely, $f;g \triangleq \bar g \circ f$.
